# UC-17.1.7 · Profiling Accuracy

## Description

Accurate device profiling enables correct authorization policies. Misprofiled devices may get inappropriate access.

## Value

Accurate device profiling enables correct authorization policies. Misprofiled devices may get inappropriate access.

## Implementation

Monitor profiling events and profile changes. Track devices that are frequently re-profiled (indicates ambiguous profiling rules). Validate profiling accuracy against known device inventory. Tune profiling policies.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_cisco-ise`, HPE Aruba ClearPass syslog, Forescout CounterACT syslog.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: ISE profiler logs, re-profiling events.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Monitor profiling events and profile changes. Track devices that are frequently re-profiled (indicates ambiguous profiling rules). Validate profiling accuracy against known device inventory. Tune profiling policies.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:profiler"
| search "re-profiled" OR "profile changed"
| stats count by endpoint_mac, old_profile, new_profile
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Profiling Accuracy** — Accurate device profiling enables correct authorization policies. Misprofiled devices may get inappropriate access.

Documented **Data sources**: ISE profiler logs, re-profiling events. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_cisco-ise`, HPE Aruba ClearPass syslog, Forescout CounterACT syslog. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: nac; **sourcetype**: cisco:ise:profiler. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=nac, sourcetype="cisco:ise:profiler". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by endpoint_mac, old_profile, new_profile** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with Cisco ISE Live Log and endpoint details for the same sessions, MACs, and time range.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Step 4 — Operationalize
Step 4 — Operationalize
Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (profiling changes), Sankey diagram (old→new profiles), Bar chart (re-profiling frequency).

## SPL

```spl
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:profiler"
| search "re-profiled" OR "profile changed"
| stats count by endpoint_mac, old_profile, new_profile
| sort -count
```

## CIM SPL

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication
  by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src span=1h
| sort - count
```

## Visualization

Table (profiling changes), Sankey diagram (old→new profiles), Bar chart (re-profiling frequency).
